---
title: "Mapping Legislative Attention to the Social Determinants of Mental Health: A Text Analysis of State Bills, 2021-2024"
author: "Jacob Federbusch"
institute: "American University"
email: "your.email@american.edu"
abstract: |
  Write your abstract here following the recommendations for an APA empirical abstract. Must not exceed 150 words, providing a concise summary of your research question, methodology, key findings, and implications. The abstract will appear in italic text at the top of the first column.
keywords: [keyword1, keyword2, keyword3, keyword4, keyword5]
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
format: 
  pdf:
    template: hicss_quarto.tex
    keep-tex: false              # Change to true if you need to troubleshoot LaTeX errors
    echo: false                  # Hides code - shows only results
    warning: false
    message: false
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: false
    code-line-numbers: false
    cite-method: citeproc
    natbib: false
    biblatex: false
execute:
  freeze: false                  # Run fresh each time for final submission
  python:
    executable: "/add/your/path/here"  # Specify your Python path here
editor: source
---

# Introduction

The social determinants of health (SDOH) represent the fundamental environmental, social, and economic conditions in which individuals are born, grow, live, work, and age that shape their overall well-being and health outcomes [@kirbySOCIALDETERMINANTSMENTAL; @SocialDeterminantsHealtha; @jesteSocialDeterminantsMental2022]. While these determinants influence both physical and psychological health, the core domains particularly relevant to mental health and this study include economic stability, education access and quality, health care access and quality, neighborhood and built environment, social and community context, and discrimination, equity, and rights [@SocialDeterminantsHealtha; @kirbySOCIALDETERMINANTSMENTAL]. Crucially, experiences of disadvantage follow a distinct social gradient, meaning that individuals occupying lower social positions face compounding mental health risks that accumulate over the life course through persistent exposure to chronic stress and structural inequities [@alegriaSocialDeterminantsMental2018]. Furthermore, this relationship is highly reciprocal, as untreated mental health disorders can actively precipitate further social disadvantage--such as unemployment, marital instability, or housing loss--creating a harmful feedback loop that deeply entrenches inequality [@rotterSocialDeterminantsMental2022; @alegriaSocialDeterminantsMental2018].

These foundational factors function as upstream drivers, acting as the fundamental causes that shape downstream living conditions and immediate health risks [@alegriaSocialDeterminantsMental2018; @ericksonTheyJustHelped2023]. Consequently, effectively addressing mental health requires a critical shift away from focusing exclusively on individual pathology and biomedical models of disease, moving instead toward an examination of the systemic inequities and structural conditions that generate psychological distress [@kirbySOCIALDETERMINANTSMENTAL; @macintyreWhatHasEconomics2018]. While individual-level clinical interventions are important, public policy serves as an important mechanism for shaping these overarching environmental and socioeconomic conditions [@kirbySOCIALDETERMINANTSMENTAL; @gostinLegalDeterminantsHealth2019]. By establishing rules, structuring societal systems, and directing the equitable distribution of resources, the law can directly mediate these upstream social determinants, providing an important tool to restructure societal frameworks and facilitate a healthier, more equitable society [@ericksonTheyJustHelped2023; @kirbySOCIALDETERMINANTSMENTAL; @gostinLegalDeterminantsHealth2019].

Despite widespread recognition of the critical importance of the SDOH, existing scholarship does not systematically map how state mental health bills address these specific domains across varying legislative and political contexts. This gap persists largely because the current literature is dominated by qualitative examples and narrow case studies that, while providing deep insights, cannot easily capture broad structural trends. These legal texts create a methodological challenge because they consist of large volumes of unstructured language that are difficult to analyze systematically through manual review [@sewerinUnderstandingPolicyDesign2023; @grimmerTextDataPromise2013].

To overcome the costs and logistical barriers of manual coding, text-as-data methods provide a scalable way to analyze large collections of political texts [@grimmerTextDataPromise2013]. By leveraging these computational techniques, researchers can extract meaningful patterns from large-scale legislative corpora that were previously difficult to process systematically at scale [@grimmerTextDataPromise2013]. In this study, legislative attention refers to the extent and pattern of social determinant language observed in state mental health bill texts. To address the current gap in the literature, this study maps legislative attention to the social determinants of mental health in a LegiScan-derived, keyword-filtered corpus of introduced state mental health bills from ten states between 2021 and 2024, comparing patterns across domains, states, session years, and party control.


# Literature Review

Mental health is shaped not only by clinical care and individual risk factors, but also by upstream social, economic, and environmental conditions. While downstream factors typically involve individual-level risks, health behaviors, and direct clinical interventions, upstream determinants represent the fundamental societal, political, and economic structures that act as the root causes of these health inequities [@whitmanAddressingSocialDeterminants; @chibaniUnravelingPolicyChallenges2025]. A substantial body of evidence demonstrates that experiences of socioeconomic disadvantage--including poverty, unemployment, housing instability, social exclusion, and unsafe physical environments--are consistently linked to worse mental health outcomes and higher rates of psychiatric illness [@kirbySOCIALDETERMINANTSMENTAL; @macintyreWhatHasEconomics2018]. Because these foundational determinants influence an individual's health and well-being through diverse, interacting pathways across the lifespan, utilizing a domain-based lens that categorizes these factors into distinct areas like economic stability, neighborhood and built environment, and social and community context provides a highly useful framework for understanding and addressing them [@SocialDeterminantsHealtha; @stormSocialDeterminantsAssociated2025].

A domain-based framework is useful because the social-determinants literature groups upstream influences into recurring categories rather than treating them as one undifferentiated factor. For instance, the Healthy People 2030 initiative organizes these conditions into five distinct domains: Economic Stability, Education Access and Quality, Health Care Access and Quality, Neighborhood and Built Environment, and Social and Community Context [@SocialDeterminantsHealtha]. These broad categories naturally map onto themes already visible in the mental health literature, where researchers consistently link psychiatric outcomes to specific upstream factors like income and job insecurity, poor education, limited care access, housing instability, and lack of social support or exposure to violence [@kirbySOCIALDETERMINANTSMENTAL; @LeveragingDataon]. Furthermore, systemic discrimination, health equity, and human rights deserve explicit attention in mental-health-focused studies because experiences of social exclusion, structural racism, and stigma act as fundamental causes of psychological distress that compound vulnerabilities across all other domains [@kirbySOCIALDETERMINANTSMENTAL; @whitmanAddressingSocialDeterminants; @macintyreWhatHasEconomics2018].

If social determinants shape mental health, then law and public policy are important mechanisms through which governments structure those conditions. Through statutory and regulatory frameworks, the law directly affects the operations of public and private institutions, dictates the equitable or inequitable distribution of resources, and shapes both eligibility for and the delivery of health services [@gostinLegalDeterminantsHealth2019]. Because of its pervasive influence, policy possesses a dual potential: it can either actively reduce harm by building strong health systems, or it can reproduce social exclusion and entrench stigma [@gostinLegalDeterminantsHealth2019]. Poorly designed or punitive laws can institutionalize inequality and create systemic barriers that discourage marginalized populations from seeking treatment [@gostinLegalDeterminantsHealth2019]. Consequently, legislative language matters immensely because policy texts do more than merely record objective administrative actions; the specific wording used actively frames societal problems, conceptualizes target populations, and signals governmental priorities and approaches to intervention [@popaModellingPolicyAction2025]. 

Existing state-level mental health scholarship suggests that attention to social determinants is real but uneven across domains and jurisdictions. As Rotter and colleagues demonstrate, state mental health authorities display broad recognition and concern regarding the relevance of social determinants, yet this awareness suffers from a lack of standardized approaches and limited intentional implementation of specific screening tools or intervention mandates [@rotterSocialDeterminantsMental2022]. Moreover, programmatic focus is highly skewed; some determinants--especially housing instability and health care access and quality--receive significantly more attention and targeted action than other crucial factors such as education, food insecurity, and discrimination, equity, and rights [@rotterSocialDeterminantsMental2022]. Consequently, this variation across states is substantively meaningful and not merely a data convenience, as these diverging regional priorities create profound inequities and dictate fundamentally different lived experiences within the mental health system depending on where an individual resides [@nelsonStatePoliciesThat2022; @rosenbergCentralisationRegionalisationDesigning2023]. 

Although prior scholarship shows that social determinants matter for mental health and that law helps structure those conditions, less is known about how state mental health bills systematically express these concerns across domains and contexts. While the existing literature thoroughly explains why SDOH matter for overall well-being and why public policy is a crucial mechanism for addressing structural health inequities [@kirbySOCIALDETERMINANTSMENTAL], current research does not fully map domain-specific language across a comprehensive, multi-stage bill corpus [@nelsonStatePoliciesThat2022; @sewerinUnderstandingPolicyDesign2023]. Furthermore, variation across states, session years, and party control--such as shifts in interparty competition, electoral turnover, and differing regional governance--are highly plausible and important factors in shaping public policy [@takedaDeterminantsImplementationState1987], but they are still underexamined in this specific legislative-text context [@popaModellingPolicyAction2025]. To address this critical gap, this study contributes to the field by mapping social determinant language in state mental health bills across domains, states, session years, and party control.

# Conceptual Framework

Because law can greatly affect the social determinants of mental health, state policy is an important site for understanding how governments respond to those conditions. This project treats state mental health bills as institutional signals of which social determinants receive legislative attention. Bill text serves as the observable link between scholarship on social determinants and measurable patterns of legislative attention. The analysis compares these patterns across states, session years, and party control, with the goal of mapping policy attention rather than estimating downstream mental health outcomes.


![This framework depicts how social determinants of mental health are translated into observable patterns of legislative attention through state mental health bills, with text-derived measures used to compare variation across states, session years, and party control.](ConceptualFramework.png){#fig-framework width="100%"}


# Research Questions

RQ1. How frequently do state mental health bills mention specific social determinant domains, and which domains are most prominent?
RQ1a. Which domain terms are most frequent by TF?
RQ1b. Which terms are most distinctive by TF-IDF?
RQ1c. Which bigrams and trigrams recur most often within each domain?

RQ2. How does the use of social determinant domain language vary by state, session year, and party control?
RQ2a. Which states use the highest share of neighborhood and built environment or discrimination, equity, and rights language?
RQ2b. Does domain language increase or decrease over time?
RQ2c. Are certain domains more common under Democratic, Republican, or divided control?

RQ3. What terms, phrases, and named entities distinguish bills with high versus low levels of social determinant language?
RQ3a. Which words and phrases are most distinctive in high-domain bills?
RQ3b. Which organizations, agencies, programs, or place names appear most often in high-domain bills?
RQ3c. Do high-domain bills reference schools, housing authorities, Medicaid, or local governments more often than low-domain bills?

RQ4. What latent themes emerge in mental health bills, and how do those themes relate to concrete social determinant domains?
RQ4a. Which topics are most common in the corpus?
RQ4b. Which topics co-occur with high neighborhood and built environment, education access and quality, or discrimination, equity, and rights scores?
RQ4c. Do some states or session years show stronger topic-domain combinations than others?


# Methodology

## Data Collection and Corpus Construction

This study draws on introduced state legislative bills from ten U.S. states—New York, Illinois, Texas, Florida, California, Pennsylvania, Washington, Georgia, Wisconsin, and Ohio—covering regular legislative sessions in the 2021–2024 study window. Bill metadata were sourced from publicly available LegiScan historical API JSON session archives downloaded from LegiScan’s dataset portal (API JSON bulk archives), which provide one JSON record per bill and include structured fields (e.g., bill number, title, description, session identifiers) plus a texts array listing available bill-text versions and their doc_id values [@legiscan].

From the downloaded session archives, a Python extraction script identified 153,200 bill JSON files and compiled a metadata table of 150,603 bill records that contained an Introduced text entry. The analysis is restricted to the introduced version to ensure comparability across bills at the same stage of the legislative process (pre-amendment). For each eligible bill, the script recorded key fields including bill_id, state, bill number, session identifiers, title, description, bill type, special-session flag, introduced-text doc_id, introduced-text MIME type, and source links. This metadata table serves as the master registry for all downstream filtering and text retrieval.

Because full bill text is not included in the bulk archives and because large-scale full-text retrieval is constrained by API limits, the candidate pool was narrowed using a fixed keyword screen applied to bill titles and descriptions. The screening logic retained bills whose title or description matched at least one term from a pre-specified list of mental-health-related keywords (e.g., “mental health,” “behavioral health,” “suicide,” “civil commitment,” “trauma-informed,” “substance use disorder”), implemented as a case-insensitive pattern match. This strategy efficiently targets bills likely to be substantively focused on mental health prior to full-text retrieval, while acknowledging the possibility of false negatives (indirectly relevant bills that do not use the screened vocabulary) and false positives (incidental mentions) [@grimmerTextDataPromise2013]. This screening step reduced the candidate pool to 3,395 mental-health-relevant bills.

For each screened bill, full text was retrieved using the LegiScan API getBillText endpoint, which returns bill text as a Base64-encoded doc field alongside a MIME type [@legiscan]. Returned Base64 strings were decoded into raw bytes and then converted to standardized UTF-8 plain text using a format-specific workflow:
- HTML sources (text/html) were decoded as UTF-8 strings, parsed with BeautifulSoup, stripped of markup, and whitespace-normalized to plain text [@beautifulsoup].
- PDF sources (application/pdf) were saved as raw binaries and then converted to text using pdfminer.six, with whitespace normalization applied post-extraction [@pdfminer].

Raw responses and binaries were preserved in a separate audit directory to preserve source material and support reproducibility. Retrieval and extraction were validated using (a) automated file-count checks, (b) an issues log for deferred or failed cases, and (c) manual spot-checking of randomly sampled plain-text files to verify readability and content integrity.

After full-text construction, the analytic corpus was restricted to regular-session bills (special sessions excluded) and to bill-type records (non-bills such as resolutions excluded). Applying these rules yielded a final analytic corpus of 3,169 introduced bill texts in UTF-8 plain-text form, paired with bill-level metadata for analysis.

A key limitation of the data collection pipeline is that PDF-to-text extraction preserves lexical content but removes visual markup (e.g., strikethrough or underlining used to denote repealed or inserted language). As a result, repealed and inserted text may be treated identically in downstream token-based analyses; this limitation is documented and carried forward as a validity consideration for interpretation [@pdfminer].

## Data Preparation

Two contextual variables—session year and party control—were prepared for subgroup comparisons in RQ2 and for descriptive stratification throughout the analysis. Session year was coded as the regular legislative session in which the bill was introduced, using the session start year provided in LegiScan metadata. All states in the sample except Florida operate on a biennial session structure, so sessions were coded as 2021 and 2023, corresponding to the first year of each regular session (2021-2022 and 2023-2024). Florida has an annual session structure, but it was coded using the same session-year variable (2021 and 2023) to maintain comparability and consistency across states.

Party control was coded at the opening of each regular session (rather than at the bill’s specific introduction date) and categorized as Democratic trifecta, Republican trifecta, or divided government, where a trifecta indicates unified party control of the governorship and both legislative chambers. Party control data were compiled from the National Conference of State Legislatures and cross-checked against Ballotpedia [@ncsl; @ballotpedia]. These contextual variables are used as descriptive comparison dimensions rather than as causal treatments.

From the master corpus table, the analysis constructs a set of derived tables used across the research questions: (1) a token table for unigram-based frequency and TF–IDF analysis, (2) an n-gram table for bigram and trigram analysis, (3) a named-entity table for organization and place extraction, (4) a domain dictionary table for Social Determinants of Health (SDOH) measurement, and (5) a topic table of document–topic proportions for RQ4. All text processing was implemented in R using the tidytext workflow for tokenization and document-term representations [@tidytext], with spacyr used for named entity recognition via spaCy’s English model [@spacyr; @spacy].

Preprocessing decisions were tailored to the measurement task. For dictionary scoring, n-grams, and named entity recognition, the analysis uses raw, unstemmed tokens to preserve interpretability and to avoid breaking exact dictionary phrase matches and entity boundaries. For TF–IDF and topic modeling, the analysis uses a more normalized representation (lemmatized tokens) to reduce sparsity from inflectional variants and to better capture recurring themes across documents.

Tokenization followed a conservative cleaning strategy designed for legislative text: standard stopwords (tidytext’s stop word lexicon) were removed, pure numbers and very short tokens were excluded, and a custom legislative stopword list was applied iteratively to remove high-frequency procedural boilerplate (e.g., “section,” “chapter,” “subsection,” “shall”) that is ubiquitous in bills but not substantively informative. For phrase analysis, bigrams and trigrams were constructed and filtered to exclude any n-grams containing stopwords or numeric tokens; a small additional list of formatting artifacts (e.g., markup language produced during bill drafting or PDF extraction) was also removed to prevent document-format conventions from dominating substantive phrase patterns.

Named entity recognition required additional preparation because spaCy enforces a default maximum document length. To avoid systematic failures on unusually long bills, texts exceeding 1,500,000 characters were truncated to the first 1,500,000 characters prior to entity extraction; these cases were limited to a small number of omnibus or budget-style bills whose substantively relevant provisions appear early in the text. The resulting entity table was then cleaned through iterative inspection of the most frequent entities to remove generic procedural artifacts (e.g., “sec,” chamber labels, and fragmented boilerplate). Entity types were restricted to substantively interpretable categories (primarily organizations and geopolitical entities). The spaCy LAW entity type was excluded from analysis because it predominantly captured internal legislative cross-references (e.g., “section 5,” “chapter 57”) and page/formatting markers rather than meaningful named statutes, yielding insufficient signal for the study’s comparative aims.

Together, these preparation steps produce a single, reproducible master corpus table plus task-specific derived tables that preserve the link between raw bill text, bill metadata, and the text-derived measures used to answer RQ1–RQ4.

## Dictionary and Measures

To operationalize “legislative attention” to upstream determinants, this study uses a custom domain dictionary that maps bill language to six social-determinant domains relevant to mental health: Economic Stability; Education Access and Quality; Health Care Access and Quality; Neighborhood and Built Environment; Social and Community Context; and Discrimination, Equity, and Rights [@SocialDeterminantsHealtha; @kirbySOCIALDETERMINANTSMENTAL]. Dictionary methods are appropriate for this purpose because they provide a transparent, deductive measurement strategy in which concept definitions are specified ex ante and then applied consistently across a large corpus [@grimmerTextDataPromise2013].

Dictionary development combined (1) deductive term selection from the social-determinants literature and the Healthy People 2030 SDOH framework and (2) an inductive validation pass using a 30-bill sample from the corpus to identify additional domain-relevant language and to remove ambiguous or overly generic terms. Candidate terms were retained only if they met two substantive criteria: (a) the term clearly referred to a social condition, structural factor, or population characteristic (rather than a clinical outcome or purely procedural legislative language), and (b) its presence plausibly distinguished a bill as addressing an upstream determinant rather than generic mental-health service delivery. As a result, several intuitively related but overly ubiquitous terms (e.g., broad healthcare terms that appear in nearly all mental-health bills) were avoided to reduce false positives and preserve domain specificity.

The final dictionary includes unigrams, bigrams, and trigrams (1–3 word terms/phrases). Multiword entries were included because many determinant concepts are expressed most clearly as short phrases (e.g., “food insecurity,” “job training,” “health equity”) that would be diluted or misclassified if measured only at the unigram level.

Dictionary matching was performed on raw, unstemmed tokens to preserve interpretability and to avoid breaking exact phrase matches (e.g., “safety net,” “structural racism”). Text was lowercased and normalized during preprocessing, and standard stopwords plus a custom legislative stopword list were removed to reduce boilerplate (e.g., “section,” “chapter,” “shall”) that is not substantively informative. Unigram matching was performed against the token table, while bigram and trigram matching was performed against n-gram tables constructed from each bill’s text using tidytext’s tokenization workflow [@tidytext]. Because bills use heterogeneous drafting conventions across states, this approach prioritizes consistent, reproducible matching rules over state-specific manual recoding.

Dictionary matches were aggregated into three bill-level measures that serve as inputs to the research questions:
1. Domain indicators (binary). For each domain, a bill was coded as mentioning that domain if it contained at least one matching dictionary term or phrase. These indicators are the basis for subgroup comparisons in RQ2, where results are reported as shares of bills within each subgroup (state, session year, or party-control category) rather than raw counts, to ensure comparability across groups of unequal size.
2. Domain scores (counts). For each domain, a bill received a domain score equal to the count of unique dictionary terms/phrases from that domain observed in the bill text. Using unique-term counts (rather than total occurrences) reduces the influence of repetitive drafting language and better reflects the breadth of domain-relevant content in a bill.
3. Overall “domain intensity” score. For analyses that require a single summary measure of upstream focus (RQ3), each bill received a total domain score equal to the sum of unique matched terms across all domains.

Importantly, domain categorization was non-exclusive: a single bill can match multiple domains, consistent with the fact that comprehensive mental health legislation may address several upstream determinants simultaneously (e.g., schooling, insurance coverage, and housing-related provisions in the same bill).

To define comparison groups for “high-domain” versus “low-domain” bills in RQ3, bills were ranked by the overall domain intensity score and split using a quartile rule. High-domain bills are those above the 75th percentile of the score distribution, and low-domain bills are those below the 25th percentile. This rule avoids an arbitrary cutoff while producing two clearly separated groups for contrastive term/phrase and named-entity comparisons.

For education-related domain language, “high-domain” bills were defined as those at or above the 75th percentile of the domain score distribution. For neighborhood and discrimination domains, scores were extremely sparse (three quarters of bills scored zero), so high-domain bills were instead defined as those with any non-zero domain score (score ≥ 1). This ensures that the high-domain group meaningfully differs from the corpus while keeping cutoffs transparent and simple.

## Analytical Approaches

All analyses treat the introduced bill text as the unit of analysis (one bill = one document) and use text-derived measures to describe and compare patterns of legislative attention rather than to estimate causal effects. Results are therefore interpreted as descriptive, contextual comparisons across states, session years, and party-control categories. Text processing and measurement were implemented primarily in R using the tidytext workflow for tidy token tables and document-term representations [@tidytext], with named entity recognition performed via spacyr (wrapping spaCy’s en_core_web_lg English model) [@spacyr; @spacy]. Topic modeling was implemented with the topicmodels package using Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) [@topicmodels].

To address RQ1 (domain prominence and recurring expressions), the analysis combines dictionary filtering with unigram and n-gram frequency measures. First, dictionary terms and phrases were linked to the token and n-gram tables (unigrams via the token table; bigrams/trigrams via the n-gram table). For RQ1a, domain-term prominence is summarized using term frequency (TF), operationalized as the total count of each dictionary term across the corpus (i.e., summing token counts across bills). For RQ1b, term distinctiveness is summarized with TF–IDF, treating each bill as a separate document so that terms frequent in a small subset of bills are up-weighted relative to terms that occur broadly across the corpus (implemented using tidytext’s TF–IDF workflow) [@tidytext]. For RQ1c, recurring phrases are measured using bigrams and trigrams constructed from bill text, filtered to remove n-grams containing stopwords and formatting artifacts, and then summarized as the most frequent domain-matched n-grams within each domain.

To address RQ2 (variation by state, session year, and party control), the primary outcome is the bill-level domain indicator from the dictionary scoring stage: for each domain, a bill is coded as 1 if it contains at least one dictionary term/phrase and 0 otherwise. Comparisons are reported as shares (proportions) within each subgroup—e.g., the share of bills in a given state-session or party-control category that mention a domain—rather than raw counts, to ensure comparability across groups of unequal size (e.g., New York vs. Ohio; annual vs. biennial session structures). Party control is treated as a contextual grouping variable coded at the opening of each regular session (Democratic trifecta, Republican trifecta, or divided government), and findings are interpreted as descriptive patterns rather than causal party effects.

To address RQ3 (what distinguishes bills with high vs. low social-determinant language), bills were first ranked by the overall domain intensity score (the total number of unique dictionary matches across domains). Bills above the 75th percentile were defined as high-domain, and bills below the 25th percentile were defined as low-domain. Distinctive language was then identified using three complementary approaches. First, unigrams and n-grams were compared across the high- vs. low-domain subsets using class-based TF–IDF, treating the high-domain subset as one aggregated document and the low-domain subset as another, so that terms/phrases characteristic of high-domain bills are up-weighted relative to low-domain bills [@tidytext]. Second, recurrent bigrams and trigrams were compared using the same contrastive logic to identify short policy-relevant expressions that separate the two groups. Third, named entity recognition (NER) was used to extract organizations and geopolitical entities that function as concrete institutional or place-based signals in high-domain bills (e.g., agencies, programs, state departments). NER was executed with spacyr/spaCy and then cleaned through iterative inspection of the most frequent entities; generic procedural artifacts were removed using a custom entity stoplist. The spaCy LAW entity type was excluded because it predominantly captured internal legislative cross-references and formatting artifacts rather than substantively meaningful named laws, yielding insufficient signal for comparison.

To address RQ4 (latent themes and their relationship to concrete SDOH domains), the analysis fit an LDA topic model to the corpus using a document-term matrix derived from the cleaned token table [@topicmodels]. Because topic models are sensitive to sparsity and inflectional variation, the topic-model input uses a more normalized representation (lemmatized tokens) than the dictionary and n-gram components. The number of topics was selected as k=20 based on (1) perplexity comparisons across candidate values (k∈{10,15,20,25,30}) and (2) substantive inspection of the top terms per topic to ensure interpretability. The fitted model produces document–topic proportions (γ) for each bill, which were then joined back to bill metadata and domain scores to examine which topics are most prevalent overall and how topic prevalence co-varies with domain indicators/scores across states and session years. Six topics were identified as procedural/formatting artifacts (state-specific header language, citation conventions, and extraction remnants) and were retained in the topic table for transparency but excluded from substantive RQ4 comparisons.

## Validation

Validation was implemented as a combination of automated audit checks during corpus assembly and targeted, method-specific validation for the dictionary, named entities, and topic model. During data collection and corpus construction, the pipeline preserved raw API responses and PDF binaries, maintained retrieval and extraction issue logs, verified file counts at each stage, and performed manual spot-checks of randomly sampled extracted texts to confirm that HTML and PDF workflows produced readable, substantively correct bill text.

The SDOH dictionary was validated through a two-stage process that combines deductive specification with inductive checking, consistent with best practices for transparent dictionary measurement in political text analysis [@grimmerTextDataPromise2013]. First, candidate terms were selected from the SDOH literature and the Healthy People 2030 domain framework, with inclusion rules requiring that each term clearly denote an upstream social condition rather than clinical symptoms or legislative boilerplate. Second, an inductive validation pass on a 30-bill sample was used to (a) identify missing high-signal terms and phrases and (b) remove or revise ambiguous entries that produced false positives in context. As a final face-validity check, extremal-case reading was used to review a small set of bills with the highest domain scores in selected domains and bills scoring zero across domains, and a focused false-positive/false-negative audit was conducted for at least one domain by manually comparing flagged versus non-flagged bills.

NER outputs were validated through model diagnostics and iterative cleaning. Entity extraction used spacyr with spaCy’s en_core_web_lg English model [@spacyr; @spacy]. Because spaCy enforces a maximum document length, very long bills were truncated prior to parsing (only two omnibus New York budget bills required truncation at 1,500,000 characters), and all 3,169 bills were successfully parsed after this adjustment. The resulting entity table was validated by inspecting high-frequency entities by type and iteratively removing procedural artifacts and generic labels (e.g., chamber terms, citation fragments, and boilerplate) using a custom stoplist. The LAW entity type was excluded from substantive analysis after diagnostic review showed systematic misclassification of internal legislative cross-references (e.g., “section 5,” “chapter 57”) and page markers as named laws, yielding insufficient signal for the study’s institutional-comparison aims. A final sample-based plausibility check compared extracted organizations and places in a small random set of bills against manual reading to assess whether dominant extracted entities corresponded to clearly referenced institutions.

The LDA model was validated at three levels: model selection, topic interpretability, and artifact handling. The number of topics was selected as k=20 by comparing perplexity across candidate values and then confirming substantive coherence through inspection of top-β terms per topic [@topicmodels; @tidytext]. After fitting, topics were reviewed for state-specific formatting and extraction artifacts; six topics were identified as non-substantive (procedural headers, citation conventions, and markup remnants) and were retained for transparency but flagged as artifacts and excluded from RQ4 comparisons. Document-level plausibility checks were then conducted by reading exemplar bills with the highest topic proportions (γ) for key substantive topics (e.g., education/community and insurance/Medicaid) to confirm that high-γ assignments aligned with the underlying bill content.

# Findings

## RQ1

Raw term frequencies show that education and healthcare access domains dominate the corpus by volume, with terms such as education (tf = 1,197), employment (tf = 535), medicaid (tf = 420), and housing (tf = 336) appearing most often across introduced bills (see @fig-rq1a-tf). By contrast, discrimination-focused language is less frequent overall, with equity (tf = 127), stigma (tf = 115), and discrimination (tf = 65) at the top of that domain. Social community terms like incarceration (tf = 136) and isolation (tf = 54) also appear at more modest levels.

![Top domain terms by frequency (RQ1a).](output/figures/rq1a_tf.png){#fig-rq1a-tf width=65%}

TF-IDF scores highlight a somewhat different pattern: within healthcare access, telehealth (mean TF-IDF ≈ 1.04) and uninsured (0.66) are especially distinctive, and in neighborhood-environment, neighborhood (1.25), pollution (1.08), and violence (0.85) stand out despite not being the most frequent raw tokens (see @fig-rq1b-tfidf). Discrimination-equity terms such as stigma (0.99), disparity (0.86), and equity (0.72), along with social-community terms like isolation (1.39) and incarceration (1.28), also emerge as highly distinctive to the subset of bills that reference them, indicating that when this language appears it tends to cluster in particular pieces of legislation rather than diffuse evenly across the corpus.

![Top domain terms by distinctiveness (TF-IDF, RQ1b).](output/figures/rq1b_tfidf.png){#fig-rq1b-tfidf width=65%}

Phrase-level patterns reinforce these findings: bigrams such as substance abuse (612 occurrences), human services (508), and managed care (215), and trigrams like behavioral health services (241), health care provider (189), and community mental health (165) recur across bills, indicating that domain language is often embedded in specific service, agency, and enforcement formulations rather than isolated single words (see @fig-rq1c-bigrams).

![Top domain bigrams in mental health bills (RQ1c).](output/figures/rq1c_bigrams.png){#fig-rq1c-bigrams width=65%}

## RQ2

State-level comparisons show that, of all states, Washington exhibits the highest shares of bills mentioning most domains, with roughly 72 percent of its mental health bills referencing healthcare access or social-community language and 58 percent mentioning education access, while Ohio stands out for economic stability language, with about 61 percent of its bills referencing that domain (see @fig-rq2-state).

![Share of mental health bills mentioning each domain, by state (RQ2a).](output/figures/rq2_state_share.png){#fig-rq2-state width=65%}

Across the 2021-2023 sessions, domain shares remain relatively stable: education access language rises only slightly from 0.42 to 0.43 of bills, economic stability from 0.328 to 0.332, and healthcare access from 0.40 to 0.42, while social-community references dip modestly from about 0.51 to 0.48. Discrimination-equity and neighborhood-environment mentions show similarly small shifts (roughly 0.11 to 0.09 and 0.22 to 0.21, respectively), suggesting no dramatic reorientation of domain emphasis over the study window. 

By party control, divided governments have the highest shares of bills mentioning economic stability (about 45 percent) and education access (about 49 percent), whereas Democratic trifecta states show the highest shares for healthcare access (0.42), neighborhood and built environment (0.22), social-community context (0.51), and discrimination-equity language (0.12) (see @fig-rq2-party).

![Share of mental health bills mentioning each domain, by party control (RQ2c).](output/figures/rq2_party_share.png){#fig-rq2-party width=65%}

## RQ3

To examine how language differs between bills with higher and lower SDOH intensity, I computed class-based TF-IDF for unigrams and bigrams in the high- and low-domain bill groups. High-domain unigrams skew toward broad policy language, including terms such as equity, sanction, prisoner, and hiv. This pattern suggests that high-domain bills are engaging more directly with problems, sanctions, and conditions, rather than narrow procedural detail. In contrast, low-domain unigrams are more idiosyncratic and proper-noun-like, with examples including welch, emanuel, chris, and tamms, alongside niche medical or procedural terms like hyperbaric, psychosurgical, and mastectomy. This suggests that low-domain bills are more heterogeneous and often anchored in specific projects, locations, or medical technologies rather than a general SDOH framing.

As with unigrams, high-domain bigrams are coherent and policy-relevant, with examples such as certified mental, health assistant, mandatory prison, and designated crisis. Together, these bigrams highlight service delivery and justice or public-safety interfaces around mental health, such as crisis response and prison settings. In contrast, low-domain bigrams are dominated by procedural and fiscal language like appropriations project, project request, and project titled. These bigrams indicate that many low-domain bills are primarily appropriations and project-funding vehicles, with less substantive SDOH language. Overall, high-domain bills show more substantively coherent SDOH-related language in both unigrams and bigrams, whereas low-domain bills mix project- and appropriations-oriented language with idiosyncratic proper nouns and medical terms.

To answer RQ3b, I compared organization (ORG) and geopolitical (GPE) entities between high- and low-domain bills using spaCy NER. I then plotted the top ORGs by total mentions per group, computed high:low mention ratios for ORGs appearing in both groups, and plotted the most frequent GPEs. In the first plot, I found that high-domain bills consistently reference state departments (e.g., Department of Public Health) and public insurance programs (e.g., Medicaid) that directly administer or finance mental health and SDOH-related services, suggesting an institutional focus on service systems and payers. Low-domain bills, by contrast, highlight codes and generic bodies (e.g., The Task Force) rather than specific service agencies or payers, indicating a more procedural emphasis and less direct SDOH content. Surprisingly, the top low-domain "ORG" is a statute reference (Health and Safety Code), not an organization, illustrating NER misclassification. The second plot reveals that there are several extreme high:low ratios, including The Illinois Department (~500:1), The Nursing Home Care Act (~140:1), The Internal Revenue Code (~125:1), The Department of Children (~95:1), and The Substance Use Disorder Act (~80:1). The entities in this plot include state agencies, public insurance programs, and several statutory or code names that spaCy mistakenly labeled as ORGs. These ratios show that when these agencies, payers, and program/act names appear at all, they are overwhelmingly concentrated in high-domain bills. Note that even the least skewed entities in the figure are still roughly 30:1-50:1, reinforcing that the ORGs shown are strongly characteristic of high-domain bills, not shared evenly. In the GPE plot, both panels are dominated by state and federal references, with only modest differences: high-domain bills reference multiple large states and federal context, while low-domain bills lean slightly more on New York-centered internal references. Overall, high-domain bills disproportionately mention public payers and state health and human-services agencies, whereas low-domain bills instead emphasize statutory codes and task forces, with more NER artifacts, underscoring that they are less centered on SDOH service systems and more on procedural or project-specific matters.

To answer RQ3c, I compared institutional reference rates for four categories (Schools, Medicaid, Local Government, Housing) in high- vs. low-domain bills, using both n-gram pattern matching and entity-based matching (spaCy ORG/GPE). To start, both methods agree that Medicaid references are on the order of ten times more common in high-domain bills. Both methods also show higher school-related references in high-domain bills, though the n-gram method shows a larger gap, with roughly two to three times the share of high-domain bills mentioning schools compared with low-domain bills. Similarly, n-grams show a very clear gap for local government, with high-domain bills around six times more likely to mention local government than low-domain bills. But the entity method is near-zero for both, suggesting local-government references are often embedded in phrases that NER doesn't tag as cleanly as ORGs. Finally, in the housing category, n-grams indicate that housing is meaningfully referenced in high-domain bills but almost never in low-domain bills, whereas entity-based detection is sparse and asymmetric, with a very low bar for high-domain bills and no bar at all for low-domain bills. Across all categories, high-domain bills have higher institutional reference rates than low-domain bills, most strikingly for Medicaid and Schools. The discrepancy between methods for Local Government and Housing underscores that dictionary-based n-grams are picking up relevant institutions that NER either under-tags or omits altogether.

# RQ4

To answer RQ4a, I used an LDA model to estimate document-topic proportions, then plotted mean $\gamma$ per topic (topic prevalence), and counted how often each topic had the highest $\gamma$ in a bill (dominant-topic counts). The top five topics by mean $\gamma$ are: (1) Education & Community (0.113), (2) Advisory Bodies & Task Forces (0.066), (3) Insurance & Medicaid Coverage (0.061), (4) Criminal Justice (0.061), and (5) Workforce Licensing (0.054). The distribution is highly concentrated, not flat. Education & Community is clearly dominant, with a mean $\gamma$ substantially higher than that of the next topic, and the top five topics have mean $\gamma$ values substantially higher than all other topics. Pre-identified SDOH-focal topics rank as: Education & Community (1st), Insurance & Medicaid Coverage (3rd), Criminal Justice (4th), and Policy Equity & Conditions (10th). Three of the four focal topics are in the upper half. The lowest substantive topics by mean $\gamma$ are Healthcare Facilities & Licensing (0.004), Drug Crime & Penalties (0.006), Corporate & Regulatory (0.009), Maternal & Prenatal Health (0.012), and Policy Equity & Conditions (0.013). This suggests that bills rarely focus on facility licensing, drug penalties, corporate regulation, or maternal/prenatal health, relative to education, advisory bodies, insurance, criminal justice, and workforce.

The dominant-topic counts (bills where the topic has the highest $\gamma$) are: Education & Community (709 bills; 22.2%), Advisory Bodies & Task Forces (415 bills; 13.1%), Insurance & Medicaid Coverage (394 bills; 12.4%), Criminal Justice (358 bills; 11.3%), Workforce Licensing (318 bills; 10.0%). These five cover 2,188 of 3,169 bills (69.0%). The top five topics are identical in both rankings, in the same order. Wrapping up RQ4a, most mental-health bills in this corpus are organized around a small set of institutional and systems-oriented topics rather than narrowly clinical or diagnostic themes. The absence of explicitly clinical-care topics among the most prevalent and dominant topics suggests that legislation is more often concerned with systems, infrastructure, and cross-sector coordination than with specific treatment protocols.

To answer RQ4b, I compared topic proportions with SDOH domain scores, producing a topic-domain heatmap of alignment (i.e., mean document–topic proportion difference between high-domain bills and the full corpus) for key domains: Education Access & Quality, Discrimination, Equity & Rights, and Neighborhood & Built Environment. For Discrimination, Equity & Rights, the strongest alignment is with Advisory Bodies & Task Forces (+0.080), with the secondary alignment in Education & Community (+0.052). This suggests that equity/discrimination concerns are most strongly associated with bills creating or empowering commissions, oversight bodies, and task forces, rather than a dedicated equity-themed topic. For Education Access & Quality, the strongest alignment is with the Education & Community topic (+0.059). This indicates that SDOH education domain maps cleanly onto the Education & Community topic. For Neighborhood & Built Environment, the strongest alignments are with Criminal Justice (+0.054) and Involuntary & Inpatient Treatment (+0.054) (tied). Secondary alignment is with Advisory Bodies & Task Forces (+0.040). This suggests that neighborhood environment concerns are most often expressed through justice-system and involuntary treatment procedures, with advisory bodies again playing a supporting role. Cross-domain, Advisory Bodies & Task Forces is strong for Discrimination/Equity (+0.080), moderate for Neighborhood (+0.040), weak but positive for Education (+0.006). This suggests that this topic includes commissions and task forces that are repeatedly leveraged across SDOH domains, especially to address equity. Involuntary & Inpatient Treatment is another cross-domain topic that shows modest to strong positive alignment across all three focal domains (+0.017 to +0.054). An explanation for this pattern is that involuntary/inpatient treatment procedures appear in bills touching multiple SDOH domains, suggesting procedural protections and confinement are a recurring cross-domain concern. One surprisingly weak alignment is with Insurance & Medicaid Coverage, which shows negative alignment (−0.013 to −0.033) with the three focal SDOH domains. In other words, high SDOH-domain scores (education, equity, neighborhood) are associated with less insurance/payer language, not more. This suggests that bills framed most strongly around education, equity, or neighborhood SDOH are not the ones that expand or emphasize payer coverage, highlighting a disconnect between SDOH framing and health-system implementation. Pulling together these findings, the heatmap shows a largely one-to-one or one-to-few mapping: education concerns align with the Education & Community topic, neighborhood concerns with Criminal Justice and Involuntary & Inpatient Treatment, and equity concerns most strongly with Advisory Bodies & Task Forces rather than a dedicated equity topic. Advisory Bodies emerges as a disproportionately central cross-domain topic, suggesting that legislatures often respond to SDOH issues—especially equity—by creating or strengthening commissions and task forces rather than by embedding SDOH language in operational program topics. 

To address RQ4c, I treated the state and year analyses as exploratory context rather than primary tests. The year trend plot shows that all four SDOH-focal topics—Education & Community, Criminal Justice, Insurance & Medicaid Coverage, and Policy Equity & Conditions—are present in each session year and change only gradually over time. Mean topic proportions increase slightly from 2021 to 2023 for Education & Community and for the other focal topics, but there is no sharp break or reversal, suggesting incremental rather than dramatic growth in SDOH framing over this short window.

At the state level, the heatmaps and state×domain interaction plot show that several topics are represented in every state but with modest differences in emphasis. Education & Community and Insurance & Medicaid Coverage are relatively prominent in larger states such as California, Illinois, and New York, while Involuntary & Inpatient Treatment and Criminal Justice are more concentrated in a subset of states, including Ohio, Washington, and Georgia. Within states, mean domain scores and aligned-topic proportions generally move in the same direction but not in lockstep, indicating that some states have stronger topic–domain alignment for particular SDOH domains than others. Given the small number of years and the limited set of states in this corpus, I interpret RQ4c descriptively: SDOH-aligned topics appear across states and years, with moderate variation in emphasis, but the data do not show a clear, uniform temporal trend or a single “leader” state in topic–domain alignment.

# Discussion

## Interpreting SDOH domain attention (RQ1 + RQ2)

Across RQ1 and RQ2, the results show that SDOH language in state mental health bills is unevenly distributed across domains. Across domains, education access and quality, health care access and quality, and social and community context language appear in the largest shares of bills in most states and in both session years, while economic stability and neighborhood and built environment are somewhat less common, and discrimination, equity, and rights is the least common. Within domains, a small set of high-frequency terms and phrases carries most of the weight. Education, Medicaid, housing, employment, and substance abuse all appear often, whereas equity, stigma, neighborhood, isolation, and other SDOH terms occur less frequently but show high mean TF‑IDF, indicating that they cluster in particular bills rather than diffusing across the corpus. Taken together, this pattern suggests that legislatures, if they're going to focus on SDOH domains to improve mental health, are most comfortable framing mental health through schools, service access, and community programs, while equity and neighborhood conditions receive more specialized and concentrated attention rather than being treated as routine elements of mental health policy.

State-level patterns suggest distinct policy profiles rather than a single national template. For example, Washington has particularly high shares of bills mentioning health care access and quality, social and community context, and neighborhood and built environment, while Ohio stands out for economic stability and neighborhood and built environment references. By party control, both Democratic trifectas and Republican trifectas share high attention to education access and quality, health care access and quality, and social and community context. But Democratic control is associated with a higher share of bills mentioning discrimination, equity, and rights, Republican control has somewhat higher economic stability shares, and divided governments show especially high shares for economic stability and education access and quality, although the number of divided-government bills is relatively small. While we cannot infer causality from these patterns, they indicate that both state context and partisan control align with different emphases within the same SDOH framework--for example, Democratic contexts bringing equity language into mental health bills more often, and divided or Republican contexts emphasizing economic stability.

## High- vs. low-domain bills (RQ3)

The high- versus low-domain contrast in RQ3 shows that SDOH intensity is tied to different kinds of distinctive bill language rather than simply more of the same terms. In the high-domain group, the top TF-IDF unigrams and bigrams include SDOH-adjacent and justice-related words and phrases such as equity, prisoner, mandatory prison, designated crisis, and degree felony, alongside environmental and implementation terms like liquid, vapor, fuel, and redevelopment project. In the low-domain group, by contrast, the most distinctive unigrams and bigrams are dominated by project scaffolding and highly specific names and technical terms--for example appropriations project, project request, nonrecurring sum, hyperbaric, mastectomy, psychosurgical, and personal or locality names such as pfas, ombudsperson, tamms, and parkway. These patterns suggest that bills with stronger SDOH dictionary scores are more likely to pair SDOH language with broader justice, crisis-response, and implementation vocabulary, whereas low-domain bills tend to concentrate distinctive language in narrow project funding, technical delivery mechanisms, or idiosyncratic locations and people rather than in upstream social conditions.

These lexical differences are mirrored in the institutional references that appear most often in each group. Among high-domain bills, the top organization entities include public payers such as Medicaid, Medicare, and Medi-Cal and state health and human-services agencies such as the Department of Human Services, the Department of Public Health, the Department of Healthcare and Family Services, and Federally Qualified Health Centers, alongside some statutory codes and acts. In the low-domain group, the most prominent organizations are fewer in number and skew toward generic task-force labels and statutory code names, such as the Health and Safety Code, the Welfare and Institutions Code, and the Task Force, with fewer distinct public payer or human-services agency names appearing among the top entities. High-to-low mention ratios reinforce this pattern: when entities like Medicaid, Medicare, and state health departments appear in both groups, they are dozens of times more common in high-domain bills than in low-domain bills. This does not mean that low-domain bills never reference agencies or that high-domain bills are free of statutory noise, but it does indicate that the bills with the densest SDOH dictionary scores are also the ones where named service payers and health agencies are most prominently and repeatedly invoked.

Geopolitical entities, in contrast, show few differences between high- and low-domain bills. Both groups frequently reference the study states themselves, the United States, and locality-specific references. This suggests that place-based language is common across the corpus and does not differentiate high- vs. low-domain bills in a meaningful way.

Finally, the institutional reference comparison for specific categories (Schools, Medicaid, Local Government, Housing) shows that high-domain bills are much more likely to reference schools and Medicaid than low-domain bills, with roughly two to three times the share of high-domain bills mentioning schools and about ten times the share mentioning Medicaid. For local government and housing, the n-gram method shows a clear gap with higher shares in high-domain bills, but the entity-based method is sparse and less consistent, suggesting that relevant references to local government and housing may be embedded in phrases that NER does not tag as cleanly as ORGs. Overall, high-domain bills show more frequent and concentrated references to key SDOH-related institutions such as schools and Medicaid, while low-domain bills are more likely to reference generic task forces and statutory codes without a strong institutional focus on service systems.

## Topics and domains together (RQ4)

The topic model results in RQ4 indicate that most introduced state mental health bills cluster around a small set of institutional and systems-oriented themes rather than being evenly distributed across many topics. Education & Community is clearly the most prevalent topic in the corpus, with the highest mean document–topic proportion and the largest number of bills for which it is dominant, followed by Advisory Bodies & Task Forces, Insurance & Medicaid Coverage, Criminal Justice, and Workforce Licensing. Lower-prevalence topics include more specialized themes such as Involuntary & Inpatient Treatment, Budget & Fiscal Provisions, Maternal & Prenatal Health, Healthcare Facilities & Licensing, and Drug Crime & Penalties. This pattern suggests that contemporary state mental health legislation in these ten states is most often organized around education settings, advisory and governance structures, coverage and financing, justice-system interfaces, and workforce regulation, with fewer bills devoted primarily to narrow clinical or facility-licensing issues.

Linking topics to SDOH domain scores clarifies where dictionary-based measures align with latent themes and where they do not. For the education access and quality domain, bills with high dictionary scores show substantially higher mean gamma for the Education & Community topic than the corpus average, and they also lean more toward Workforce Licensing and several related governance topics such as Policy Equity & Conditions and Licensing & Privacy Records. This provides a face-validity check for the education domain measure: bills coded as education-focused by the dictionary are also those where the Education & Community topic is most prominent. More unexpectedly, bills with high discrimination, equity, and rights scores show the strongest positive gamma differences for Advisory Bodies & Task Forces and Policy Equity & Conditions, with notable increases as well for Education & Community, Corporate & Regulatory, and Healthcare Facilities & Licensing. Rather than coalescing into a single “equity” topic, equity-related language appears in bills that emphasize commissions, task forces, advisory structures, and cross-cutting conditions, suggesting that legislatures often route equity concerns through institutional oversight bodies and cross-sector governance mechanisms.

Neighborhood and built environment domain scores show yet another pattern. High neighborhood-domain bills display elevated proportions not only of Criminal Justice but also of Involuntary & Inpatient Treatment, Advisory Bodies & Task Forces, and several regulatory and facility-related topics. This indicates that neighborhood and environmental concerns in mental health bills are frequently intertwined with justice-system procedures, involuntary treatment provisions, and broader regulatory or facility-governance language rather than being captured in a dedicated “neighborhood” topic. At the same time, Involuntary & Inpatient Treatment and Advisory Bodies & Task Forces show positive gamma differences across more than one focal domain, implying that these topics play cross-domain roles—appearing more often in bills with high education, neighborhood, and equity dictionary scores—rather than mapping neatly onto a single SDOH category.

A notable cross-cutting result is the weak or negative alignment between the Insurance & Medicaid Coverage topic and bills with high scores in the three focal SDOH domains. For neighborhood and built environment, education access and quality, and discrimination, equity, and rights, the Insurance & Medicaid topic has negative gamma differences, meaning that bills with high SDOH-domain scores tend to devote less of their text to insurance and coverage language than the corpus as a whole. This suggests a disconnect between bills that are strongly framed around upstream educational, neighborhood, or equity concerns and bills whose dominant focus is on payer and coverage arrangements, at least within this ten-state corpus. Taken together, the topic–domain patterns imply that SDOH framing in state mental health legislation is most tightly linked to education and community contexts, advisory and oversight bodies, justice and involuntary treatment procedures, and cross-cutting equity conditions, while traditional insurance and coverage topics are comparatively less emphasized in the subset of bills that score highest on these SDOH domains.

Although topic–domain combinations vary somewhat across states and session years, the short time window and limited set of states mean these differences are treated as contextual rather than as strong temporal trends or state rankings.

## Implications and Recommendations

The domain and topic results suggest that state mental health bills most often frame upstream determinants through education, health-care access, and social and community context, while discrimination, equity, and neighborhood conditions appear less frequently and more concentrically in specific bills. Legislatures and advocates aiming to advance mental health equity could use these findings to press for more routine incorporation of equity and neighborhood language into mainstream mental health legislation not just in specialized commissions or justice bills.

Bills with the highest education-, neighborhood-, and equity-domain scores tend to devote less text to Insurance & Medicaid Coverage topics than the corpus average, despite strong over-representation of Medicaid and other public payers among high-domain organization entities. This disconnect suggests an opportunity for drafters to more explicitly tie upstream SDOH framing to concrete payer, coverage, and reimbursement language so that equity, education, and neighborhood concerns are more fully integrated into the mechanisms that finance and deliver services.

Equity-related language shows its strongest topic alignment with Advisory Bodies & Task Forces and Policy Equity & Conditions, indicating that commissions and task forces are a common vehicle for addressing discrimination, equity, and rights in this corpus. These structures can play a valuable coordinating role, but the patterns here also point to the importance of pairing advisory mechanisms with statutory changes to programs, funding, and enforcement if legislatures want equity considerations to move beyond consultation into implementation.

The project demonstrates that combining dictionary methods with topic modeling and NER can surface meaningful patterns in legislative attention, but also highlights measurement gaps, especially around discrimination and neighborhood language. Future monitoring of mental health legislation could build on this workflow by iteratively refining SDOH dictionaries and entity lists, using periodic human review to adjust categories as legislative language evolves.

## Limitations

This project has several limitations that shape how its findings should be interpreted. First, the SDOH domain dictionary is a necessary simplification of a much richer conceptual space; even with literature-based term selection, inductive corrections, and targeted validation checks, some relevant language is missed and some borderline phrases are misclassified, especially in domains such as discrimination and neighborhood conditions. Second, the corpus itself is restricted to ten states, introduced bill versions, and regular sessions between 2021 and 2024, meaning that the domain patterns observed here may not generalize to other jurisdictions, bill types, or time periods. Third, off‑the‑shelf models for named entities and topic modeling introduce artifacts: spaCy mislabels some statutes and formatting as organizations and places, and several LDA topics capture procedural or formatting conventions rather than substantive themes despite efforts to flag and exclude them from key comparisons. Finally, the comparative analyses by state, session year, and party control are descriptive rather than causal, so observed differences should be read as patterns of legislative attention, not as evidence that partisan control or state context directly causes changes in SDOH language.

# Future Research

Future research could extend this analysis in several ways. First, expanding the corpus to additional states, a longer time window, and other kinds of mental-health-adjacent bills (such as appropriations or regulatory amendments) would make it possible to assess whether the domain and topic patterns observed here generalize beyond the ten-state sample and 2021–2024 sessions. Second, richer SDOH measurement strategies—such as semi‑supervised dictionary expansion, supervised classifiers trained on hand-coded SDOH labels, or hybrid approaches that combine dictionary terms with contextual embeddings—could improve coverage of discrimination, equity, and neighborhood language while reducing false positives. Third, institutional reference measures could be sharpened by developing domain-specific NER models for agencies, programs, and statutory instruments, building on but moving beyond generic ORG and GPE categories that can misclassify statutes and formatting as substantive entities. Finally, linking the legislative attention patterns documented here to implementation or outcome data—for example, budget allocations, program launches, or changes in service coverage—would allow future work to move from mapping how SDOH appear in bill text toward understanding how those textual patterns relate to real-world changes in mental health systems, even if strong causal claims remain out of scope.

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
